title: [Bank] IG02 — high CPU usage
prediction: {"root cause occurrence datetime": "2021-03-09 01:38:00", "root cause component": "Redis01", "root cause reason": "Analyzing the full dependency graph and telemetry data, Redis01 is the most likely root cause service for this incident. Key indicators: (1) Redis01 shows extreme cumulative counters at the earliest observable timestamps \u2014 total_commands_processed at 5.02B, keyspace_hits at 3.82B, rdb_changes_since_last_save at 1.11B, and total_connections_received at 32.09M, all reported at or near 01:38:00, dwarfing Redis02's equivalent counters despite Redis02 being healthy (\ud83d\udfe2). The rdb_changes_since_last_save value of 1,109,463,695 is especially alarming \u2014 this indicates a massive volume of dirty writes pending persistence, suggesting Redis01 was under extreme write pressure and potentially approaching memory saturation given maxmemory is capped at 4294967296 bytes (~4GB) while used_memory_peak is suspiciously low at 50,413,208, which may indicate an eviction storm or memory reporting anomaly. (2) Redis01's NETPacketsIn (5.68B) and NETPacketsOut (5.16B) at 02:36:00 are orders of magnitude higher than IG01/IG02's packet counts (~402M/~403M), confirming Redis01 was absorbing disproportionate traffic load, consistent with a cache stampede or hot-key scenario. (3) The Tomcat layer (Tomcat01\u201304) all show JVMMaxMemory and JVMTotalMemory hitting 4187619328 bytes (fully allocated heap) with ProcessingTime accumulating into the hundreds of millions, and Tomcat01 standing out with 957M processing time \u2014 roughly 5x that of Tomcat02\u201304 \u2014 suggesting it was blocked waiting on a downstream dependency, most likely Redis01 or a Redis-backed session/cache lookup. (4) The MG01/MG02 middleware gateways and IG01/IG02 ingress gateways are all \ud83d\udd34 but show stable host uptime and no OS-level distress, consistent with cascading latency from a downstream cache layer rather than an independent failure in those services. (5) Mysql01 and Mysql02 are \ud83d\udfe2 and show only minor slow query activity (max 0.73s query time), ruling out the database layer as the origin. Mysql02's InnoDB page_cleaner warning at 01:55:13 (5440ms loop) is a secondary symptom of elevated write pressure, likely propagated from application retries caused by Redis01 degradation. (6) Apache01's filesystem metrics are nominal (no exhaustion), and its access logs show normal HTTP 200 responses, confirming the web tier is not the origin but is experiencing elevated latency passed up from the application stack. (7) Tomcat02's GC logs show healthy ParNew collections (sub-100ms) with no Full GC events, suggesting JVM pressure is load-driven rather than a memory leak \u2014 consistent with application threads piling up waiting on a slow cache. The failure propagation chain is: Redis01 (cache overload / connection saturation) \u2192 Tomcat01\u201304 (thread pool exhaustion from blocked Redis calls, heap pressure) \u2192 MG01/MG02 (request queuing at middleware) \u2192 IG01/IG02 (ingress timeout/retry amplification) \u2192 apache01 (upstream \ud83d\udd34 alert fires). The fault began at the earliest anomalous data point: 2021-03-09 01:38:00 CST, when Redis01 counters already show extreme accumulated values. No deployment event is directly visible in the telemetry, but the k6 load testing tool (k6/0.29.0) is explicitly present in both apache01 and Tomcat access logs, strongly suggesting an active load test was the trigger \u2014 either intentional performance testing or a runaway load generator that overwhelmed Redis01's connection and command throughput capacity."}
